jsonLdWeb.name: Wall Framing Calculator
jsonLdWeb.description: Calculate wall framing materials: studs, plates, and headers for doors and windows.

mainEntity.name: How many studs do I need for a wall?

acceptedAnswer.text: For 16" on-center spacing: multiply wall length in feet by 0.75, then add 1 for the end. Add 2 king studs and 2 jack studs per opening, plus cripple studs above and below windows. A typical rule of thumb is 1 stud per linear foot plus 10-15% for waste and extras.

mainEntity.name: What is the difference between 16" and 24" on-center framing?

acceptedAnswer.text: 16" OC (on-center) means studs are spaced 16 inches apart center-to-center, which is standard for load-bearing walls and most residential construction. 24" OC spacing uses fewer studs and is allowed for non-load-bearing interior walls and some energy-efficient designs (Advanced Framing / OVE).

mainEntity.name: What are king studs and jack studs?

acceptedAnswer.text: King studs run full height from bottom plate to top plate on each side of an opening. Jack studs (trimmers) are shorter studs that support the header — they run from the bottom plate up to the bottom of the header. Each opening requires 2 king studs and 2 jack studs (one of each per side).

mainEntity.name: How do I size a header?

acceptedAnswer.text: Header size depends on the opening width and load above. General guidelines: up to 4 ft opening = double 2×6, up to 6 ft = double 2×8, up to 8 ft = double 2×10, up to 10 ft = double 2×12. For load-bearing walls with significant loads above, consult an engineer or local code span tables.

mainEntity.name: Why do I need a double top plate?

acceptedAnswer.text: The double top plate ties wall sections together and distributes loads from above. The second plate overlaps joints in the first plate and connects intersecting walls. Some advanced framing techniques use a single top plate with metal connector plates at joints, but double top plate is standard practice.

mainEntity.name: How many plates do I need?

acceptedAnswer.text: Standard wall framing uses 3 plates per wall run: 1 bottom (sole) plate and 2 top plates (double top plate). Total plate length = wall length × 3. For a 20-foot wall, you need 60 linear feet of plate material. Use treated lumber for the bottom plate on concrete slabs.

Header Sizing Reference

General header sizes for load-bearing walls. Always verify with local building codes and engineering for specific conditions.

Opening WidthHeader SizeJack Studs Each SideNotes
Up to 4'Double 2×61Standard door/window
4' to 6'Double 2×81Wide window
6' to 8'Double 2×102Patio door / large window
8' to 10'Double 2×122Garage door (single)
10' to 12'Engineered / LVL2+Engineer required
12'+Steel or LVL beamPer engineerEngineer required
howItWorks:
How Wall Framing Is Calculated

Wall framing calculations determine the number of studs, plates, and headers needed. Standard residential framing uses 2×4 or 2×6 studs at 16" on-center.

Studs = (Wall Length × 12 ÷ Spacing) + 1 + Opening Extras
  • Regular studs: wall length in inches ÷ spacing + 1
  • Per opening: 2 king studs + 2 jack studs + cripple studs
  • Cripples above: opening width ÷ spacing (above header and below sill for windows)
  • Corner posts: 3 studs per corner or wall intersection
  • Plates: wall length × 3 (1 bottom + 2 top)
  • Subtract studs displaced by openings from regular stud count
faqHTML:
Frequently Asked Questions

How many studs do I need for a wall?

For 16" OC spacing: multiply wall length in feet by 0.75, then add 1 for the end. Add 2 king studs and 2 jack studs per opening, plus cripple studs. A common rule of thumb is about 1 stud per foot plus 10-15% for waste and extras.

What is the difference between 16" and 24" on-center?

16" OC is standard for load-bearing walls and most residential construction. 24" OC uses fewer studs and is allowed for non-load-bearing interior walls and some advanced framing (OVE) techniques in energy-efficient construction.

What are king studs and jack studs?

King studs run full height on each side of an opening. Jack studs (trimmers) support the header from below. Each opening needs 2 king studs and 2 jack studs (one of each per side).

How do I size a header?

Header size depends on opening width and load: up to 4 ft = double 2×6, up to 6 ft = double 2×8, up to 8 ft = double 2×10, up to 10 ft = double 2×12. Consult local codes for load-bearing walls.

Why is a double top plate needed?

The double top plate ties wall sections together and distributes loads. The second plate overlaps joints in the first and connects intersecting walls. Single top plates are used in some advanced framing with metal connectors at joints.

How many plates do I need?

Standard framing: 3 plates per wall (1 bottom + 2 top). Total plate length = wall length × 3. For a 20 ft wall, that is 60 linear feet. Use treated lumber for the bottom plate on concrete slabs.
